title: Essential hypertension

sections.heading: Summary
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Summary

Hypertension is defined as office systolic ≥130 mmHg or diastolic ≥80 mmHg on two or more occasions, or current use of antihypertensive medication. Most cases are primary (essential); secondary causes must be considered when clues suggest an underlying disorder.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Epidemiology

- Prevalence increases with age; over half of adults ≥60 years are affected
- Higher rates in Black adults and among patients with obesity, diabetes, or chronic kidney disease
- Often asymptomatic until end-organ damage occurs

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Pathophysiology

Increased peripheral vascular resistance is the dominant hemodynamic abnormality in established hypertension.

- Early: increased cardiac output and sympathetic activity
- Late: arteriolar remodeling, endothelial dysfunction, sodium retention
- Neurohormonal contributors: RAAS activation, sympathetic tone, vascular stiffness

sections.heading: Clinical presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Clinical presentation

Most patients are asymptomatic. Seek symptoms suggesting secondary causes or hypertensive emergency.

| Finding | Suggests |
| --- | --- |
| Resistant BP | Secondary hypertension, nonadherence |
| Hypokalemia | Primary aldosteronism |
| Abdominal bruit | Renal artery stenosis |

sections.heading: Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Diagnosis

Confirm elevated readings outside the office when feasible. Evaluate for end-organ damage and secondary causes when indicated.

1. Repeat measurements on separate visits
2. Home or ambulatory BP monitoring when white-coat effect suspected
3. Basic labs: BMP, lipids, urinalysis, ECG

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Management

Lifestyle modification is first-line for all patients. Pharmacotherapy is indicated when BP remains above goal despite lifestyle changes or when baseline risk is high.

- Weight loss, DASH diet, sodium restriction, regular aerobic exercise
- First-line agents: thiazide-like diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, or CCBs
- Combine agents from different classes when monotherapy is insufficient

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Complications

Untreated hypertension accelerates atherosclerosis and target-organ injury.

- Stroke and transient ischemic attack
- Left ventricular hypertrophy and heart failure
- Chronic kidney disease and proteinuria
- Hypertensive retinopathy

sections.heading: Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## Pearls

- Start one drug at a low dose and titrate; adherence matters more than drug class in many patients
- Do not miss medication-induced hypertension (NSAIDs, decongestants, stimulants)

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
## References

- ACC/AHA blood pressure guidelines
- JNC and subsequent consensus statements on BP classification

preambleMarkdown:
Essential hypertension is persistently elevated blood pressure without an identifiable secondary cause. It is the leading modifiable risk factor for stroke, myocardial infarction, heart failure, and chronic kidney disease.

> [!hy] Blood pressure targets should be individualized based on comorbidities and age.
